title: Measure lengths to nearest 1/4 unit
description: Use a ruler to measure lengths to the nearest 1/4 unit.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the height of the sunflower?**  

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] feet
hints:
The height starts at $0\text{ ft}$ on the ruler.  

Where does the height end?
The height ends after $5\text{ ft}$ but before $6\text { ft}$.

To figure out the exact length, we can compare a ruler to a fraction bar.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can use equivalent fractions and label the ruler in simplest form.

[[☃ image 1]]

Now, we can look at where the height ends on the ruler.


[[☃ image 1]]
The sunflower's height is $5\dfrac{1}{4}$ feet.

options.alt: A sunflower is measured against a ruler. The ruler extends from 0 to 6 feet. Each foot is labeled and the space between each foot is divided into 4 equal sections by 3 evenly spaced unlabeled tick marks. The sunflower measures to the first unlabeled tick mark after 5.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the width of the bookcase?**  

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] feet
hints:
The width starts at $0\text{ ft}$ on the ruler.  

Where does the width end?
The width ends after $4\text{ ft}$ but before $5\text { ft}$.

To figure out the exact length, we can compare a ruler to a fraction bar.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can use equivalent fractions and label the ruler in simplest form.

[[☃ image 1]]

Now, we can look at where the width ends on the ruler.

[[☃ image 1]]
The bookcase's width is $4\dfrac{1}{2}$ feet.

options.alt: The bookcase is measured against a ruler. The ruler extends from 0 to 5 feet. Each foot is labeled and the space between each foot is divided into 4 equal sections by 3 evenly spaced unlabeled tick marks. The bookcase measures to the second unlabeled tick mark after 4.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the height of the basketball equipment?**  

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] yards
hints:
The height starts at $0\text{ yd}$ on the ruler.  

Where does the height end?
The height ends after $3\text{ ft}$ but before $4\text { ft}$.

To figure out the exact length, we can compare a ruler to a fraction bar.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can use equivalent fractions and label the ruler in simplest form.

[[☃ image 1]]

Now, we can look at where the height ends on the ruler.

[[☃ image 1]]
The equipment's height is $3\dfrac{3}{4}$ yards.

options.alt: A basketball goal is measured against a ruler. The ruler extends from 0 to 5 yards. Each yard is labeled and the space between each yard is divided into 4 equal sections by 3 evenly spaced unlabeled tick marks. The basketball goal measures to the third unlabeled tick mark after 3.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the width, in inches, of the basketball?**  

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] inches
hints:
The width starts at $0\text{ in}$ on the ruler.  

Where does the width end?
The width ends after $9\text{ in}$ but before $10\text { in}$.

To figure out the exact length, we can compare a ruler to a fraction bar.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can use equivalent fractions and label the ruler in simplest form.

[[☃ image 1]]

Now, we can look at where the width ends on the ruler.

[[☃ image 1]]
The basketball's width is $9\dfrac{1}{4}$ inches.

options.alt: A basketball is measured against a ruler. The ruler extends from 0 to 10 inches. Each inch is labeled and the space between each inch is divided into 4 equal sections by 3 evenly spaced unlabeled tick marks. The basketball measures to the first unlabeled tick mark after 9.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the height of the tower?**  

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] yards
hints:
The height starts at $0\text{ yd}$ on the ruler.  

Where does the height end?
The height ends after $4\text{ yd}$ but before $5\text { yd}$.

To figure out the exact length, we can compare a ruler to a fraction bar.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can use equivalent fractions and label the ruler in simplest form.

[[☃ image 1]]

Now, we can look at where the height ends on the ruler.

[[☃ image 1]]
The tower's height is $4\dfrac{3}{4}$ yards.

options.alt: A tower is measured against a ruler. The ruler extends from 0 to 5 yards. Each yard is labeled and the space between each yard is divided into 4 equal sections by 3 evenly spaced unlabeled tick marks. The tower measures to the third unlabeled tick after 4.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the width, in inches, of the tennis ball?**  

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] inches
hints:
The width starts at $0\text{ in}$ on the ruler.  

Where does the width end?
The width ends after $2\text{ in}$ but before $3\text { in}$.

To figure out the exact length, we can compare a ruler to a fraction bar.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can use equivalent fractions and label the ruler in simplest form.

[[☃ image 1]]

Now, we can look at where the width ends on the ruler.

[[☃ image 1]]
The tennis ball's width is $2\dfrac{1}{2}$ inches.

options.alt: A tennis ball is measured against a ruler. The ruler extends from 0 to 4 inches. Each inch is labeled and the space between each inch is divided into 4 equal sections by 3 evenly spaced unlabeled tick marks. The tennis ball measures to the second unlabeled tick mark after 2.

hints: First, let's locate $2\dfrac{3}{4}\text{ in}$ on the ruler. It will be between $2$ and $3$.

We can compare a ruler to a fraction bar.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can use equivalent fractions and label the ruler in simplest form.

[[☃ image 1]]

Here is $2\dfrac{3}{4}\text{ in}$ on the ruler.

[[☃ image 1]]

All of the pencils begin at $0\text{ in}$ on the ruler.  So, we can look at where our pencils end on the ruler to find their length.

[[☃ image 1]]

Which pencil ends at $2\dfrac{3}{4}\text{ in}$?
Pencil $\redE{A}$ measures $2\dfrac{3}{4}\text{ in}$.

options.alt: Four pencils are measured against a ruler. The ruler extends from 0 inches to 4 inches. Each inch is labeled and the space between each inch is divided into 4 equal sections by 3 evenly spaced unlabeled tick marks. The pencils measure as follows: pencil A measures to the third unlabeled tick mark after 2, pencil B measures to the second unlabeled tick mark after 2, pencil C measures to the first unlabeled tick mark after 2, and pencil D measures to the tick mark at 2.

hints: First, let's locate $3\dfrac{1}{2}\text{ in}$ on the ruler. It will be between $3$ and $4$.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 1]]

Which pencil ends at $3\dfrac{1}{2}\text{ in}$?
Pencil $\blueE{B}$ measures $3\dfrac{1}{2}\text{ in}$.

options.alt: Four pencils are measured against a ruler. The ruler extends from 0 inches to 4 inches. Each inch is labeled and the space between each inch is divided into 4 equal sections by 3 evenly spaced unlabeled tick marks. The pencils measure as follows: pencil A measures to the tick mark at 4, pencil B measures to the second unlabeled tick mark after 3, pencil C measures to the first unlabeled tick mark after 3, and pencil D measures to the second unlabeled tick mark after 2.
